nativeRange.habitat: Found at the bases of steep outer reef slopes, typically at depths of 27 to 45 metres, where it hovers above mixed coral and rubble.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical Western Pacific outer reef slopes with mixed coral and rubble substrate, where small aggregations typically form in the water column just above the bottom.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs for retreat

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied carnivore diet of vitamin-enriched frozen mysis, frozen brine shrimp and other finely chopped meaty seafoods alongside a quality marine flake/pellet; small frequent feedings (2-3 times daily) tend to maintain coloration and condition.

avoidWith: triggers and large angelfish that may bully them
predatory fish that could eat a small wrasse
multiple males of the same species in a single tank

summary: A small, peaceful Western Pacific flasher wrasse known for vivid male courtship displays; generally considered reef-safe, beginner-friendly, and well suited to peaceful community reef tanks with a tight lid.
careNotes: The Carpenter's Flasher Wrasse is a small species — typically 7 to 9 cm — found on the outer reef slopes of the Western Pacific, including the Philippines, Taiwan, Indonesia, and Tonga. It is generally regarded as one of the more beginner-friendly wrasses: peaceful, broadly considered reef-safe, and willing to accept prepared marine foods readily. Like other flasher wrasses, it is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite and forms harems in the wild. Aquarists typically keep one male with a small group of two to five females, an arrangement that tends to encourage the male's intensified 'flashing' courtship coloration. If keeping multiple individuals, it is generally advised to introduce the females first and to house only one male per tank, as males are likely to fight. A tight-fitting lid or mesh top is essential — flasher wrasses are well-known jumpers and have been reported lost from open-topped tanks. Most care recommendations suggest a minimum of around 55 to 60 US gallons (roughly 208 to 227 litres) for a single specimen, with a larger volume advised to comfortably accommodate a harem alongside open mid-water swimming space.
breedingNotes: The Carpenter's Flasher Wrasse spawns in pairs, with males performing elaborate courtship displays at twilight during which the coloration of the dorsal and anal fins intensifies dramatically. Captive spawning is occasionally reported, but rearing the small pelagic larvae is generally considered difficult and is not routinely accomplished in home aquaria.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary species product page (located from collection root https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/wrasse-reef-safe). Provided care level (Easy), reef-safe (Yes), temperament (Peaceful), max size (3.5 in / ~8.9 cm), minimum tank (55-60 US gal), water parameters (72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12), diet (carnivore — mysis/brine/marine flake), and the haremic grouping recommendation (introduce females first).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific authority (Randall & Lubbock, 1981), family Labridae, order Eupercaria, max length 8.0 cm TL, depth range 27-45 m, native distribution (Philippines, Taiwan, Iriomotejima, northern Bali, Flores, Tonga), preferred temperature range 23.9-28.2°C (mean 27.2°C), oviparous with distinct pairing, IUCN Data Deficient.

additional.name: Reef Chasers care guide
additional.notes: Third-source confirmation of peaceful temperament, reef-safe (with occasional small-invert nipping), minimum 55 US gal with live rock and swim space, jumper risk requiring a netted lid, carnivorous diet accepting prepared foods, and the 'only one male per tank when keeping multiples' rule.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive spawning is occasional; rearing the small pelagic larvae is generally considered difficult and not routinely accomplished in home aquaria.
